text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST — RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old female with history of pancreatic head/uncinate process adenocarcinoma, status post pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with R0 resection, adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (completed approximately 9 months prior). Previously confirmed hepatic segment VI metastasis on MRI with gadoxetate disodium and biopsy-proven metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma, accompanied by two small bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 7 mm, left lower lobe 5 mm), all identified approximately 3 months ago. Currently on cycle 3 of palliative systemic chemotherapy with gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane). Evaluation for treatment response. CA 19-9 at prior restaging 67 U/mL, CEA 4.8 ng/mL.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the uneventful administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Acquisition was performed in portal venous phase through the chest; triphasic technique (late arterial, portal venous, and delayed phases) was employed through the abdomen and pelvis to assess the liver. Images were reconstructed at 2.5 mm axial, coronal, and sagittal reformations. Comparison made to prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 3 months prior as well as MRI liver (gadoxetate disodium) performed approximately 3 months prior.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. Lung parenchyma is well aerated without consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion. There is no pneumothorax.

Pulmonary Nodules: A 7 mm pleural-based solid nodule in the right lower lobe (series 3, image 112) is redemonstrated, unchanged in size, morphology, and attenuation compared to the prior examination approximately 3 months ago. A 5 mm solid nodule in the left lower lobe (series 3, image 108) is similarly unchanged. No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No pulmonary masses.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is unremarkable. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. Heart size is normal. The thoracic aorta is of normal caliber. No pericardial effusion. The visualized esophagus and trachea are unremarkable.

Chest Wall and Pleura: No pleural thickening or effusion. Bony thorax is intact without lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for osseous metastasis.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. A 1.2 cm hypodense focus in hepatic segment VI (series 5, image 47) is again identified. This lesion is unchanged in size (1.2 cm; previously 1.2 cm on prior CT and 1.2 cm on prior MRI liver) and demonstrates similar attenuation characteristics to the prior examination. On the delayed phase images, the lesion remains hypoattenuating relative to background hepatic parenchyma without evidence of interval growth. No new hepatic lesions are identified in the remaining hepatic parenchyma. No biliary ductal dilatation. The hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis appears patent without proximal ductal dilatation.

Pancreas / Pancreaticoduodenectomy Site: Expected post-Whipple surgical anatomy is present with absence of the pancreatic head, duodenum, and gallbladder. Residual pancreatic body and tail are visualized with associated parenchymal atrophy and mild peripancreatic fibrotic stranding at the pancreaticojejunostomy anastomotic site, unchanged from prior study. The pancreatic neck margin region demonstrates linear soft tissue density consistent with post-surgical fibrosis; no discrete mass or nodularity is seen to suggest local recurrence. The pancreatic duct in the body/tail is not dilated. The pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis appears intact without peripancreatic fluid collection or drainable abscess.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: The bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable without focal nodules or masses.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size with symmetric nephrogram. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses or calculi identified.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The gastrojejunostomy anastomosis is patent. Small bowel loops are normal in caliber and distribution. No bowel obstruction. The colon and rectum are unremarkable. No wall thickening suspicious for involvement.

Vasculature: The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and portal venous system (hepatic veins, portal vein, superior mesenteric vein, splenic vein) are patent. The superior mesenteric artery and superior mesenteric vein in the region of the prior resection bed demonstrate expected post-surgical changes without new soft tissue encasement or luminal narrowing to suggest local vascular recurrence.

Lymph Nodes: No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the periportal, peripancreatic, mesenteric, para-aortic, or pelvic regions. The largest visualized lymph node measures approximately 7 mm in the portocaval region, unchanged and within the range of normal.

Peritoneum and Omentum: The peritoneal surfaces and omentum appear smooth without nodularity, soft tissue implants, or infiltrative soft tissue density to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.

Bones (Visualized): No new lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. Visualized lumbar vertebrae, pelvis, and proximal femora are intact.

PELVIS:

The urinary bladder is partially distended and unremarkable in wall thickness. The uterus and adnexa appear atrophic, consistent with patient age and postmenopausal status. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic free fluid.

IMPRESSION:

1. Stable hepatic segment VI lesion measuring 1.2 cm, previously biopsy-confirmed metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma — no interval growth or development of additional hepatic metastases.

2. Bilateral pulmonary nodules are stable: right lower lobe 7 mm and left lower lobe 5 mm, unchanged in size and morphology compared to prior examination approximately 3 months ago, consistent with known metastatic foci.

3. No new sites of metastatic disease identified in the liver, lungs, lymph nodes, peritoneum, or osseous structures.

4. Post-pancreaticoduodenectomy surgical changes are stable with no imaging findings to suggest local tumor recurrence at the resection bed or vascular recurrence.

5. No new hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or lymphadenopathy.

Overall, the findings are consistent with radiographically stable metastatic disease in the setting of ongoing palliative gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel therapy. Correlation with interval CA 19-9 and clinical assessment is recommended.

REPORTING RADIOLOGIST: Attending radiologist, Abdominal Imaging and Intervention
EXAMINATION REVIEWED AND VERIFIED.
